mnemonics.title: Disk Scheduling Algorithms
mnemonics.content: FCFS: FIRST COME FIRST SERVED - Requests served in arrival order; SSTF: SHORTEST SEEK TIME FIRST - Choose nearest; SCAN: SWEEPS LIKE ELEVATOR - Goes end-to-end then reverses; C-SCAN: CIRCULAR SCAN - One direction then jump back; LOOK: LIMITED ELEVATOR - Like SCAN but only as far as last request.

mnemonics.title: File Allocation Methods
mnemonics.content: CONTIGUOUS: Contiguous blocks - fast random access but suffers from external fragmentation; LINKED: Linked list - flexible but no random access; INDEXED: Index block - good random access but needs extra index block.

mnemonics.title: Free Space Management
mnemonics.content: BIT VECTOR: Bitmap - one bit per block; LINKED LIST: Pointers connect free blocks; GROUPING: First free block stores addresses of other free blocks; COUNTING: Stores starting block and count of consecutive free blocks.

acronyms.usage: Disk scheduling algorithm that processes requests in arrival order
Disk scheduling algorithm that picks the nearest request
Disk scheduling that sweeps across disk in both directions
Disk scheduling that services in one direction then jumps back
Technology combining multiple disks for reliability/performance
Windows file system with journaling support
Data transfer between CPU and storage devices

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an elevator in a building that stops at every floor (track) going UP, then changes direction and goes DOWN servicing waiting passengers. The head moves continuously in one direction, servicing all requests, then reverses.
Think of a parking lot where each car (file) must park in consecutive spots. Easy to find your car but if spots are scattered, you might not find space.
Think of a treasure hunt where each clue (block) points to the next location. You must follow the chain sequentially - cannot jump directly to the end.
Think of a book table of contents - the index block is like the table listing page numbers (pointers) to each chapter (file block), allowing direct access to any chapter.
Think of a librarian's desk - frequently referenced books (disk blocks) are kept on the desk (memory) so patrons don't have to go to the stacks (disk) every time.

analogies.analogy: Like a vinyl record player needle - to play a song at the end, the needle must physically move across the record. The time to move is seek time, just like disk head seek time.
Like using a storage unit when your house (RAM) is full. You move less frequently used items (inactive memory) to the storage unit (swap space) to make room for items you need now.
Like a seating chart in a theater - each seat (disk block) has a status (occupied/free) marked in the chart (bit vector), making it easy to find available seats.
Like keeping both original and copy of important documents - if one is lost or damaged, the other still has the data. Requires twice the storage but provides backup.
Like a parking lot where cars leave gaps everywhere - finding 3 consecutive empty spots becomes impossible even though total free space exists. Must defragment to compact.
